name: Shepherd Mountain
short_description: Rockhound Shepherd Mountain near Pilot Knob for Ozark quartz and jasper specimens. Moderate terrain with seasonal access on public land.
description: Shepherd Mountain sits in Missouri's Iron County within the Salem Plateau region of the Ozark Uplift, an area rich in Precambrian and Paleozoic geology. The mountain's 1485-foot elevation offers moderate slopes ideal for rockhounding without extreme difficulty. This location attracts collectors seeking quality quartz crystals, jasper nodules, and chert formations typical of the mid-Ozark ridges. The site's mixed forest and rocky outcrops provide good exposure of the underlying dolomite and sandstone formations that host collectible specimens. Best visits happen during cooler months when weather is stable and footing is safest on the moderate slopes.
history: Shepherd Mountain's mineral deposits formed during the Precambrian to early Paleozoic periods when tectonic uplift and erosion exposed the ancient crystalline and sedimentary rock layers beneath the Ozark Plateau. Localized silica-rich groundwater percolating through dolomite and sandstone layers created the quartz crystals and chalcedonic specimens found here today, a process typical of mid-continent Ozark geology.
tips: Search along hillside outcrops and eroded wash banks where quartz clusters and jasper nodules weather out naturally - do not hammer fresh rock. Bring a hand lens to identify quality specimens in the field; many smaller quartz points and jasper pieces are worth collecting but brown-stained or fractured pieces are common. Work the moderate slopes methodically from base to ridgeline, as gravity naturally concentrates heavier specimens downslope. Early morning visits after rain often reveal freshly exposed material on trail banks.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1485 feet with moderate slopes covered in mixed deciduous forest and rocky outcrops. Terrain is uneven with exposed roots, loose rock, and scattered boulders typical of Ozark ridges. Walking distance from parking area is approximately 0.5 to 1.5 miles depending on access point. Footing requires sturdy hiking boots and careful attention to loose stone and steep sections.
best_season: April - June and September - November. Spring and fall offer cooler temperatures, stable weather, and good visibility without summer heat or winter muddy conditions. Summer heat and humidity make midday collecting uncomfortable; winter rain creates slick footing on rocky slopes.
written_directions: From Pilot Knob, take US Route 67 north approximately 3 miles. Watch for Iron County Forest Road access points or parking pullouts on the east side of the highway near mile marker 245. Shepherd Mountain is accessed via local forest service roads; check with Iron County Ranger District office in Ironton for current access conditions and directions. GPS coordinates 37.60646, -90.643455 will route you to the general location. Park in designated pullouts and hike upslope toward visible rocky outcrops.
road_conditions: Forest service access roads are unpaved and gravel-surfaced; passenger vehicles can reach main trailhead parking but high-clearance or awd vehicles recommended after heavy rain. Expect washboard and occasional ruts.
parking_notes: Limited pullout parking available near forest service road access points. No formal lot; park in designated clearings off road. Capacity approximately 3-4 vehicles. Arrive early during weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines for national forest land. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collect only loose material and weathered specimens; do not hammer or break fresh outcrops. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries marked by survey posts.
rules:

nearest_services: Pilot Knob, approximately 3 miles south. Gas, food, and supplies available in nearby Ironton (8 miles south). No water sources at the site; bring adequate supply.
accessibility_notes: Moderate uneven terrain with loose rock, exposed roots, and steep sections. Sturdy hiking boots required. Not wheelchair accessible. Terrain requires good balance and leg strength. Shaded forest canopy provides some heat relief but no shelter. Walking trails are informal and unmarked; navigation skill helpful.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Shepherd Mountain near Pilot Knob?
faq.answer: Shepherd Mountain is known for quality quartz crystals, jasper nodules, chert flakes, and feldspar specimens. The Ozark geology here produces clear to milky quartz points and banded jasper pieces ranging from thumbnail to hand-sized specimens. Most collectors find multiple specimens during a half-day visit if they search methodically along the hillside outcrops and wash banks.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Shepherd Mountain free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Shepherd Mountain is located on national forest land with free public access. No permit is required, but you must follow collection rules: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, and no commercial collection. Collect only loose specimens; hammering fresh rock is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound at Shepherd Mountain in Missouri?
faq.answer: April through June and September through November are ideal. Spring and fall provide cool temperatures, stable weather, and good visibility without summer humidity or winter muddy slopes. Avoid July-August heat and potential winter ice on the moderate slopes.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Shepherd Mountain?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on national forest land at Shepherd Mountain. Keep your dog under control and watch for snakes, especially in warmer months. Bring water for your dog and stay alert to wildlife encountered on the hillside terrain.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding at Shepherd Mountain?
faq.answer: Basic tools include a backpack, hand lens for specimen inspection, sturdy hiking boots, and a collection bag. Most collectors find loose material and weathered specimens without hammers. Bring sunscreen, water, and a map since cell service is spotty. A walking stick helps on steep uneven slopes.

faq.question: Is Shepherd Mountain a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Shepherd Mountain is beginner-friendly. The moderate terrain and abundant loose specimens make it accessible to new collectors. Kids age 8 and up can participate with adult supervision. The site rewards patience and methodical searching without requiring technical climbing or specialized equipment. Free public access eliminates cost barriers.

meta_description: Find quartz, jasper, and chert at Shepherd Mountain near Pilot Knob, Missouri. Free rockhounding on national forest land. Best April-November.
